one_line_takeaway: GPTAid turns unreliable raw LLM output into trustworthy API security rules by validating each rule through sanitizer-caught runtime errors on LLM-generated violation code, lifting precision from 12% to 92% and surfacing 210 real, developer-confirmed bugs across 8 C libraries.
key_finding: On the ground-truth dataset Dgt (200 APIs from 8 libraries, 404 ground-truth APSRs), GPTAid generated 311 APSRs at 92.3% precision and 71.0% recall (F1 0.80) for only $0.12/API. The ablation is the strongest evidence for the pipeline's value: directly using the raw LLM gives just 12% precision (F1 0.21); adding validation raises precision to 79% (F1 0.74); adding refinement reaches 92% precision (F1 0.80). GPTAid substantially outperforms prior tools on the comparison set Dcomp (306 bugs): it produced 53 APSRs / 243 bugs vs Advance's 7 APSRs / 99 bugs (~6x more APSRs), Goshawk's 8 APSRs / 10 bugs, and IPPO's 0 bugs. Applied to 47 real applications (431 APIs, 579 APSRs), it found 210 previously-unknown security bugs, 150 confirmed by developers; 61.3% of generated APSRs had no explicit documentation description (76 confirmed by library developers). Notably, directly prompting the stronger GPT-4 did WORSE than the GPTAid-over-GPT-3.5 pipeline (GPT-4 direct: 0.21 precision / 0.67 recall), reinforcing that the validation/refinement scaffolding, not raw model strength, drives accuracy.
core_contribution: The paper presents GPTAid, an automated framework that uses an LLM to generate API Parameter Security Rules (APSRs) directly from library API *source code* and then uses those rules to detect API-misuse bugs. Its two named novel contributions are (1) an execution-feedback-checking approach that validates whether an LLM-generated APSR is correct by generating 'Right code' (Cr) that calls the API cleanly, then LLM-modifying it into 'Violation code' (Cv) that breaks the rule, and confirming the rule only if executing Cv triggers an API-related runtime error caught by a sanitizer; and (2) a code differential analysis step that refines overly-general natural-language APSRs into concrete, machine-actionable ones by diffing Cr vs Cv, grouping violation codes that share the same runtime error, and having the LLM extract the shared key operation. Concrete APSRs are compiled into CodeQL detection queries. GPTAid is positioned as the first work to generate APSRs from API source code using an LLM. NOTE ON SCOPE: Advance, Goshawk, and IPPO appear extensively but are prior-work BASELINES the authors compare against, not part of GPTAid's contribution.
threat_model: Defensive/bug-finding, not offensive. The security problem being addressed is API misuse: developers calling third-party library APIs while violating parameter security constraints (e.g., passing NULL, negative sizes, using a freed/closed handle, failing to release a resource). Such violations cause memory-safety and availability bugs. The paper grounds this empirically: from 100 randomly selected known misuses in prior work, 71% stem from APSR violations; detected bugs are CWE-classified as NULL pointer dereference (CWE-476), double free (CWE-415), and improper resource shutdown/release (CWE-404), with impacts including crashes, memory corruption, DoS, and potential code execution. GPTAid is a detector that helps prevent these; it does not model an adversary. A secondary, internal 'threat' the method must handle is the LLM itself producing incorrect (hallucinated) or overly-general rules, which the validation and refinement stages exist to filter.
limitations: Author-acknowledged: (1) correctness rests on dynamic analysis, so rules whose violations do not raise a captured exception are missed -- explicitly logic errors and lock-missing-unlock bugs (the one APIMU4C bug it missed was lock-missing-unlock); monitors focus only on memory issues. (2) Detection uses intra-procedural CodeQL only, missing bugs needing inter-procedural analysis (also the main FP source, 45.2% of detection FPs, when a NULL check lives in another function) and causing 5 missed bugs vs Goshawk. (3) Right-code generation fails for ~6.5% of APIs, and when it fails ALL APSRs for that API are lost (13.7% of FNs). Reviewer-observed: results depend on a single now-dated model (gpt-3.5-turbo-0613); detection templates for the 5 patterns are hand-built, limiting rule types actually usable downstream; ground truth and all 210/311 correctness judgments were manually assessed by the authors (no external annotators reported), and the same team both generated and evaluated; bug-detection precision is 77.2%, acceptable-for-static-analysis rather than high; 88% of Dcomp APSRs are absent from docs, which favors GPTAid over the documentation-based Advance baseline by construction.

datasets_used: Ccode: corpus of 8 library source trees, 8,123 APIs / 2.53M LOC (OpenSSL, SQLite3, libpcap, libxml2, libevent, libzip, zlib, libcurl)
Dgt: ground-truth APSR set, 200 APIs (25 randomly chosen per library) with 404 manually-constructed ground-truth APSRs
Dcomp: comparison set of 306 bugs and 58 APSRs (86 bugs from Advance + 10 from Goshawk + GPTAid's, Linux-kernel bugs excluded)
APIMU4C: standard API-misuse benchmark, 12 bugs within scope
Dapp: 47 real applications (up to 10 per library, all >1,000 GitHub stars) for in-the-wild detection
quantitative_results: APSRs generation on Dgt: 92.3% precision, 71.0% recall, F1 0.80, $0.12/API, 311 APSRs generated (24 FPs, 117 FNs)
Ablation (Table VII): raw LLM 0.12 precision / F1 0.21; +Validation(Sv) 0.79 / 0.74; +Validation+Refinement(Sv+Sr) 0.92 / 0.80
Component effectiveness: raw APSRs generation recall 84.4% (2858 raw APSRs); APSRs validation precision 96% / recall 80.2%; APSRs refinement accuracy 91.5%
Right-code generation success rate 93.5%; 9/10 post-training-cutoff 'new' APIs successfully exercised
In-the-wild detection: 431 APIs -> 579 APSRs -> 210 unknown bugs, 150 developer-confirmed; detection precision 77.2%; 11/12 APIMU4C bugs detected
Documentation impact: 61.3% of generated APSRs absent from docs, 76 confirmed by library developers; 88.0% of Dcomp APSRs not explicitly documented
Direct-LLM baselines: raw GPT-3.5 accuracy 11.9%; direct GPT-4 recall 0.67 / precision 0.21 (both worse than GPTAid pipeline)
APSR taxonomy: 8 categories (5 prior + 2 new action types + Others), counts C1-C7 = 17/90/3/7/9/85/92 plus Others 8
baselines_compared: Advance (documentation-based, CodeQL): 7 APSRs / 99 bugs on Dcomp (GPTAid ~6x more APSRs)
Goshawk (source-code, alloc/dealloc-focused): 8 APSRs / 10 bugs on Dcomp
IPPO (client-code path-pair inconsistency): 0 bugs detected on Dcomp
Directly using LLM without the pipeline (gpt-3.5-turbo-0613, and separately GPT-4) as ablation/exploration baselines
